findings.description: _is_expired returns age < ttl, so it reports fresh entries as expired and expired entries as fresh — the comparison is inverted and the cache's entire TTL contract runs backwards.

Consuming the inverted _is_expired, get() deletes fresh local entries and serves genuinely expired ones from the else branch, so the cache evicts good data instantly and hands back stale data forever.
pickle.loads on bytes fetched from Redis is insecure deserialization — anyone able to write the L2 store (shared/poisoned/compromised Redis) gets arbitrary code execution.
pickle.dumps stores arbitrary Python objects in Redis with no allow-list or signing, establishing the untrusted payload that line 60 then blindly deserializes.
The in-process _local dict is mutated (del/assign) across get/set/invalidate with no locking, so concurrent access races and the del here can raise KeyError if two threads evict the same key.
stats.local_entries is written only in set(), but get() mutates _local (eviction on line 54, promotion on line 61) without updating it, so the dataclass field is stale/inconsistent after any read.
Both an L1 hit and an L2-promotion increment stats.hits identically, so the metrics cannot distinguish local hits from Redis fetches and under-report effective L2 cost.
warm() returns len(entries) (the input size) rather than the number actually persisted, so the documented 'number of entries written' is a lie whenever a set() fails or no-ops.
The RedisClient.setex stub silently discards ttl_seconds, so the L2 TTL contract the class advertises is never actually enforced.
No guard against non-positive ttl_seconds (e.g. ttl=0), leaving expiry behavior undefined at the boundary independent of the inverted comparison.
